coredata.dc:title: Heteroatoms-doped carbon dots as dual probes for heavy metal detection
coredata.dc:creator: K. Algethami, Faisal
Abdelhamid, Hani Nasser
coredata.dc:description: The utilization of l-cysteine in hydrothermal synthesis allows for the manufacture of carbon dots (CDs) that are doped with heteroatoms including oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur (N, S, O-doped CDs). CDs have a particle size ranging from 1 to 3 nm, with an average particle size of 2.5 nm. N, S, and O-doped CDs display a blue fluorescence emission at a wavelength of 425 nm. It shows a reliance on the specific excitation wavelength between 320 and 500 nm. It has a selective quenching effect specifically with copper (Cu2+) ions when exposed to interactions with heavy metal ions, as compared to other metal ions. The assay has a limit of detection (LOD) of 2 μM and exhibits a linear correlation within the concentration range of 10–33.3 μM. The fluorescence mechanism was elucidated by employing various analytical techniques, such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM , UV–Vis spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, and conductometry. An analysis of the data reveals that Cu2+ ions exhibit a strong attraction to the external surface of N, S, and O-doped CDs, leading to the formation of aggregates. N, S, and O-doped CDs can be also used as probes for electrochemical investigations utilizing cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), chronoamperometry (CA), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to produce Nyquist and Bode plots. The electrochemical results offer substantiation for the interaction between Cu2+ ions and N, S, and O-doped CDs. Zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, i.e. CDs, can improve the detection of heavy metals using optical and electrochemical methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanomaterials
Carbon dots
Heavy metals
Electrochemical
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and UV–visible spectroscopy: selective detection of Cu2+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University

Al-Imam Muhammad Ibn Saud Islamic University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported and funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University ( IMSIU ) (grant number IMSIU-RG23097 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for N, S, and O-doped CDs for heavy metal ions interaction and fluorescence and electrochemical measurements.
TEM and HR-TEM images for a-b) CDs and c-d) Cu@CDs.

a) Emission of CDs upon excitation with different wavelengths, b) the relationship between the emission wavelength and excitation wavelength, inset image shows 2D plot of the relationship, c) Fluorescence emission of CDs with different heavy metals, and d) selectivity toward other metals.

a) CDs solution under ambient and ultraviolet light without and with heavy metals, b) fluorescence emission of CDs upon interaction with different concentrations of Cu2+ ions, and d) linear relationship between the emission intensity and concentration of Cu2+ ions, inset image represents 2D plots of the intensity changes upon interaction with different concentration of Cu2+ ions.
a) current value extracted from cyclic voltammetry over scan rate, b) CV curves for different electrodes, c) current and selectivity in terms of current, and d) chronoamperometry.

a) Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves, b) Nyquist plots, c-f) electrochemical measurements upon the interaction of CDs and different Cu2+ ions using c) current obtained from CV curves at different scan rates, d) current response, e) LSV, and f) Nyquist plots.

head.ce:title: Heteroatoms-doped carbon dots as dual probes for heavy metal detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU), Riyadh, 11432, Saudi Arabia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Assiut University, Assiut, 71575, Egypt.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of l-cysteine in hydrothermal synthesis allows for the manufacture of carbon dots (CDs) that are doped with heteroatoms including oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur (N, S, O-doped CDs). CDs have a particle size ranging from 1 to 3 nm, with an average particle size of 2.5 nm. N, S, and O-doped CDs display a blue fluorescence emission at a wavelength of 425 nm. It shows a reliance on the specific excitation wavelength between 320 and 500 nm. It has a selective quenching effect specifically with copper (Cu2+) ions when exposed to interactions with heavy metal ions, as compared to other metal ions. The assay has a limit of detection (LOD) of 2 μM and exhibits a linear correlation within the concentration range of 10–33.3 μM. The fluorescence mechanism was elucidated by employing various analytical techniques, such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM , UV–Vis spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, and conductometry. An analysis of the data reveals that Cu2+ ions exhibit a strong attraction to the external surface of N, S, and O-doped CDs, leading to the formation of aggregates. N, S, and O-doped CDs can be also used as probes for electrochemical investigations utilizing cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), chronoamperometry (CA), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to produce Nyquist and Bode plots. The electrochemical results offer substantiation for the interaction between Cu2+ ions and N, S, and O-doped CDs. Zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, i.e. CDs, can improve the detection of heavy metals using optical and electrochemical methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are various classifications of carbon-based nanoparticles [1–11]. CDs possess the smallest dimensions among the three entities, with a diameter that is less than 10 nm [12–14]. CDs are frequently manufactured using organic substances e.g., glucose, citric acid [15,16], aromatic compounds [17], or biomass waste [18,19] by various synthesis procedures [20,21]. Current synthesis procedures show promising future for CDs synthesis in large scale [22]. CQDs exhibit a diameter ranging from 2 to 10 nm, which is somewhat greater than that of CDs. They possess an amorphous structure. CQDs are frequently fabricated using materials that are commonly employed in the production of CDs. CQDs can also be synthesized utilizing inorganic materials, such as carbon nanotubes or graphene. GQDs exhibit the greatest size among the three, characterized by a diameter ranging from 2 to 20 nm [23]. It consists of a singular layer of carbon atoms that are organized in a hexagonal lattice structure [24]. It possesses a precisely defined crystalline arrangement and exhibits exceptional mechanical strength and flexibility [25] with tunable chemical properties [26]. Carbon nanomaterials with 0D have advanced several applications including environmental fields [27–30], energy [31], light-emitting diode (LED) [32], biosensing [33,34], anti-counterfeiting [35], food analysis [36], biomedical applications [37,38] including drug delivery [39], and water monitoring [40]. They can be proceeded into commercial products [41,42] with minimal toxicity compared to inorganic QDs [43–46].

Heteroatom-doped CDs refer to a specific class of carbon nanomaterials that have atoms such as nitrogen and sulfur atoms integrated inside their carbon frameworks [47,48]. They exhibit a diameter of less than 10 nm and possess a quasi-spherical structure [49]. They exhibit strong fluorescence properties and possess exceptional water dispersibility, rendering them very desirable for a diverse array of applications [17]. The introduction of nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and oxygen (O) atoms into CDs has been shown to have a substantial impact on enhancing their photoluminescence (PL) characteristics [8,9,50,51]. These heteroatoms result in the creation of additional energy levels that facilitate the transfer of electrons from their ground state to an excited state [9]. Furthermore, they have the potential to effectively neutralize surface imperfections on CDs, hence leading to a subsequent enhancement in their PL intensity. Thus, CDs have demonstrated a diverse array of possible uses such as bioimaging, cancer treatment, sensing, catalysis, and optoelectronics [49,52–54].

Copper-based materials are a vital micronutrient for both human beings and animals [55]. They are implicated in various significant functions. Copper element serves as a cofactor for numerous enzymes that play a crucial role in energy production, including cytochrome C oxidase [56]. The process of iron metabolism involves the essential role of copper in facilitating the absorption and utilization of iron [57]. The neurological function of copper encompasses its role in the synthesis of neurotransmitters, including dopamine and norepinephrine [58]. Copper has a crucial role in the synthesis of white blood cells and antibodies, hence contributing to the immune response [58]. It plays a crucial role in the synthesis of collagen and elastin; essential constituents of bone structure. The formation of melanin, responsible for the pigmentation of the skin, necessitates the presence of copper. Copper insufficiency may give rise to a variety of health complications, including anemia [55]. The insufficiency of copper can give rise to neurological impairments, encompassing tremors, cognitive decline, and impaired concentration abilities. Copper has been identified as a potential factor contributing to the development of bone disorders, specifically osteoporosis. The insufficiency of copper can result in many dermatological conditions, including dermatitis, alopecia, and low response toward vaccination [55]. The suggested daily copper intake for adult individuals is 900 μg for males and 750 μg for females [59]. Copper is present in a diverse range of food sources, such as shellfish, liver, nuts, seeds, whole grains, and dark chocolate [59]. It may also be consumed in the form of a dietary supplement. Thus, it is essential to find a method to determine copper in water [60,61].

The present investigation involved the synthesis of CDs doped with nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur i.e., N, S, and O-doped CDs by a solvothermal technique. N, S, and O-doped CDs were utilized as a probe to investigate the presence of copper (Cu2+) ions in water. Our investigation involved the use of fluorescence emission and electrochemical techniques, e.g., cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), chronoamperometry (CA), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). These techniques included the analysis of Nyquist plots and Bode plots. The materials exhibit a pronounced affinity for Cu2+ ions relative to the other investigated ions i.e. sodium (Na+), cadmium (Cd2+), lead (Pb2+), and cobalt (Co2+) ions. Fluorescence spectroscopy and electrochemical analytical methods, including CV, LSV, CA, and EIS, can be utilized to quantitatively analysis of Cu2+ ions. They are promsing analytical methods for analyte detection [62–65].
l-cysteine, cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), copper sulfate (CuSO4), cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Germany).

The N, S, and O-CDs were synthesized using the process of thermal treatment of a single precursor. A solution of l-cysteine (2 g) was prepared in ultrapure water (10.0 mL) using an ultrasonication bath (30 min). The solution was transferred to a 100 mL autoclave lined with Teflon and subjected to heating at a temperature of 200 °C for 24 h. Following the completion of the reaction, the reactor was allowed to undergo natural cooling until it reached ambient room temperature. The brown-yellow substance underwent additional purification through filtration using nitrocellulose filters with a pore size of 0.22 μm. The solution containing CDs can be concentrated using a rotary evaporator. It was kept at a temperature of 4 °C. The N, S, and O-doped CDs were dispersed in ultrapure water to create a stock solution with a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1.

A stock solution of metal ions was prepared in deionized water with a concentration of 1 mM of each metal salt. The solutions were used for measurements using the appropriate volume. They are stored at room temperature.
The fluorescence spectra of N, S, and O-doped CDs solution were measured using an excitation wavelength of 254–500 nm, with band slits of 20 nm for emission measurement.

The experimental procedure involved transferring 50 μL of N, S, and O-doped CDs solution (1 mg mL−1), 1.5 mL of deionized water, and a volume of metal ion solution into a 2 mL Eppendorf tube. The solution was vigorously agitated and subsequently to a reaction duration of 3 min before measurements at ambient temperature. The fluorescence spectra were measured at excitation wavelength range of 350 nm. The fluorescence emission intensities of CDs at 425 nm were utilized to quantitatively analyze Cu2+ ions.

The electrochemical techniques employed including CV, LSV, CP, and EIS were conducted using the Corrtest® C350 instrument (Wuhan, China). A three-electrode setup consisting of a working electrode, a platinum electrode, and a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl) was employed. The working electrodes were prepared via casting N, S, and O-doped CDs (1 mg mL−1) solution into graphitic carbon electrode (GE, 1 × 2 cm2). The electrodes were immersed in a 1 mM electrolyte solution containing potassium hexacyanoferrate (III). N, S, and O-doped CDs solution was deposited with and without heavy metal ions (Cu2+ and a mixed solution containing (Cu2+, Na+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ (1 mM))

Fig. 1 illustrates the process of synthesizing heteroatom-doped CDs by the use of l-cysteine. The application of hydrothermal treatment to l-cysteine results in the formation of a solution containing brown-colored CDs. The inclusion of functional groups such as amino (―NH2), carboxylic (―COOH), and thiol (―SH) facilitates the production of CDs that are doped with nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S), respectively. In addition, these functional groups can be active binding sites for analytes, such as metal ions.

Fig. 2 displays TEM and HR-TEM images of CDs. The TEM image of the CDs reveals the presence of diminutive, opaque particles measuring between 1 and 3 nm in size, with an average particle size of 2.5 nm (Fig. 2a). This finding confirms the production of colloidal nanoparticles with a particle size of less than 10 nm i.e., CDs. The HR-TEM image of CDs reveals the presence of lattice fringes, which exhibit interparticle planes of 0.2 nm that can be attributed to the Miller index of (200). TEM and HR-TEM images are employed to investigate the hydrothermal treatment of l-cysteine, which results in the process of pyrolysis and carbonization, leading to the formation of nanoparticles.

Synthesis of CDs is a straightforward procedure with only one precursor. Green CQDs (G-CQDs) were synthesized using the latex of Ficus benghalensis and polyethyleneimine as a source of carbon and nitrogen, respectively [66]. l-cysteine in our procedure offers the source for N, C, and S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and UV–visible spectroscopy: selective detection of Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The evaluation of the optical characteristics of CDs may be conducted through the utilization of fluorescence (Figs. 3 and 4
), ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis, Fig. S1) spectra, zeta potential and conductivity (Fig. S2). N, S, and O-CDs exhibits an emission characteristics that were dependent on excitation (Fig. 3). CDs have fluorescent properties, specifically showing a unique absorption peak at around 280 nm. This absorption peak is ascribed to the electronic transition known as n–π* inside the carbon nanomaterials. The observed absorption peak is likely attributed to the phenomenon of excited state energy being captured by surface states, hence leading to a notable fluorescence response [7,67]. Fig. 3a-b demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of N, S, and O-CDs displayed an initial rise followed by a subsequent decline when the excitation wavelength was increased. The highest level of fluorescence intensity was seen when the excitation wavelength was set at 350 nm as shown in Fig. 3a-b. Additionally, it was noted that the emission peaks of CDs had a red shift as the excitation wavelength was raised from 320 to 380 nm. The behaviors that have been observed may be possibly ascribed to the size of CDs and the existence of surface states on the CD's surface. Fig. 3b illustrates a linear correlation between the excitation and emission wavelengths throughout the range of 320–500 nm.

The emission spectra of CDs displayed a peak fluorescence emission at a wavelength of 450 nm when exposed to luminescence stimulation at 350 nm (Fig. 3c and d). An insignificant change or quenching effect was observed upon contact with various metal ions (Fig. 3c and d). The fluorescence emission of CDs remains unaltered when exposed to metal ions, including Na+ and Cd2+ ions. On the contrary, the presence of heavy metal ions such as Cu2+, Pb2+, and Co2+ results in the suppression of fluorescence emission of CDs (Fig. 3c and d). These metal ions are responsible for inducing a Turn-Off process in the emission of CDs. The quenching percentage of several metal ions was analyzed, revealing that Cu2+ ions demonstrate the greatest recorded value of 80%. This finding suggests that CDs display a strong preference for Cu2+ over other species that were tested (Fig. 3c and d).

The alterations in color of the CD solution before and after its interaction with various metal ions were visualized by camera images as seen in Fig. 4a. Under normal lighting conditions, the solution of CDs has a brown color. No discernible alteration in color is observed when exposed to metal ions such as sodium (Na+), cobalt (Co2+), lead (Pb2+), and cadmium (Cd2+). On the contrary, the solution comprising both CDs and Cu2+ exhibits the characteristic hue of copper (II) salt, namely a blue tint. Moreover, the CD particles undergo aggregation when they come into contact with Cu2+ ions. Upon exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light with a wavelength of 365 nm, a distinct and intense blue luminescence may be noticed in a water-based solution containing CDs. The solution exhibits a cessation of activity upon exposure to Cu2+ ions, thereby demonstrating a notable degree of selectivity (Fig. 4a). The alteration in the solution's color likewise exhibits a progressive pattern, contingent upon the concentration of Cu2+ ions present inside the solution (Fig. S3). This discovery demonstrates the potential of CDs for conducting quantitative investigation of Cu2+ ions.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs following their contact with varying concentrations of Cu2+ ions have been documented, as seen in Fig. 4b and c. The fluorescence emission of CDs exhibits a steady decline when they come into contact with Cu2+ ions. A linear correlation can be observed between the emission intensity and the concentration of Cu2+ throughout the range of 10–33.3 μM, as depicted in Fig. 4b. The limit of detection (LOD) is determined to be 2 μM. The aforementioned data suggest that CDs can be utilized in qualitative and quantitative analyses of Cu2+ ions, exhibiting a notable level of selectivity when compared to other metal ions that were tested.

The mechanistic examination of the interaction between Cu2+ ions and CDs was evaluated using various analytical methods including TEM and HR-TEM images (Fig. 2c and d). Additionally, UV–Vis spectroscopy is utilized to analyze the samples (Fig. S1), while zeta potential and conductivity measurements are conducted (Fig. S2). Based on the naked eye observation depicted in Fig. 4a, it can be observed that the presence of Cu2+ ions leads to the agglomeration of CD particles, resulting in the formation of a precipitate. The observed aggregation can be mostly attributed to the interaction between Cu2+ ions and the functional groups of CDs (Fig. 1). The TEM image reveals larger particles of Cu2+@CDs, with a particle size of 5 nm (Fig. 2c). It is worth mentioning that the lattice fringes of CDs remain unchanged, suggesting that the interaction between Cu2+ ions and CDs occurs only on the exterior surface of the particles (Fig. 2d). The HR-TEM image of Cu2+@CDs reveals an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the Miller index (200) commonly observed in carbon nanomaterials. Both TEM and HR-TEM images indicate the absence of any discernible dark particles corresponding to copper oxide e.g., Cu2O or CuO. This observation suggests that the metal ions in the Cu2+ analyte remain in their original condition i.e., ions (Fig. 2c-d).

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching encompasses two fundamental processes, namely dynamic quenching and static quenching. The differentiation of these processes may be achieved by a meticulous analysis of the absorption spectra of the CDs fluorophore, as seen in Fig. S1. The CDs have notable absorption characteristics throughout the wavelength range of 200–400 nm. Notably, a discernible absorption peak at 280 nm was observed (Fig. S1a). The observed peak can be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl (CO) groups in CDs [68]. In the context of fluorescence quenching, it can be shown that static quenching leads to obvious alterations in the absorption spectra of the fluorophore associated with the CDs. Conversely, dynamic quenching does not result in any discernible changes in the absorption spectra. The absorption of CDs is significantly affected by the contact with certain metal ions, namely Cu2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+. This interaction results in a noticeable shift in the absorption pattern, with an increase observed in the absorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions (Fig. S1b). The validity of this discovery is contingent upon the concentration of Cu2+ ions (Fig. S1c). The absence of an observation about the absorption band of Cu2+ ions can be attributed to the low concentration of the analyte inside the solution. The interaction between copper ions and the ground state of CDs is seen by UV–Vis spectroscopy. In summary, the mechanism is mainly due to static quenching.

The zeta potential and conductivity of CDs are measured both before and after their contact with heavy metal ions (Fig. S2). The solution provided by CDs exhibits a negative surface alteration, as shown by a zeta potential value of −25.3 ± 1.0 mV. This value elucidates the mechanism behind the establishment of a stable colloidal solution of the components. Previous research has established that a stable colloidal solution containing nanoparticles typically exhibits a zeta potential ranging from −25 to +25 mV. The zeta potential values of the CDs solution were observed to be −16.5 ± 1.5 mV, −11.3 ± 1.7 mV, and −0.41 ± 0.2 mV upon contact with metal ions of Cu2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, respectively (Fig. S2). The electrostatic interactions between heavy metal ions and CDs are facilitated by the positive charge on the former and the negative charge on the latter. Consequently, the zeta potential of CDs exhibits a change from negative to positive values after their contact with heavy metal ions. The veracity of this discovery may be substantiated by the analysis of the conductivity data (Fig. S2). The average conductivity values for CDs, Cu2+@CDs, Cd2+@CDs, and Pb2+@CDs were determined to be 7.75, 8.44, 3.39, and 0.11 mS/cm, respectively (Fig. S2). The observed significant interaction between CDs and Cu2+ ions resulted in an enhancement of the electrical conductivity of CDs. On the other hand, the presence of additional metal ions leads to a reduction in the conductivity of the material (Fig. S2).

The electrochemical properties of CDs that were placed onto graphitic carbon electrodes (GE) were assessed via several analytical techniques, including CV, LSV, CA, and EIS. The CDs solution may be applied directly onto the surface of GE at room temperature, providing an electrode for conducting electrochemical experiments. GE is used as an electron collector to quantify the alterations in the electrochemical reaction of CDs in the presence and absence of heavy metal ions. The evaluation of heavy metal ions, namely Cu2+ ions, and a mixed metal ions solution consisting of Cd2+, Na+, Co2+, and Pb2+, was also conducted following the same approach.

The CV curves for four different electrodes were obtained and are presented in Fig. S4. These samples include GE (Fig. S4a), CDs decorated graphite electrode (CD@GE) (Fig. S4b), copper-modified CD@GE (Cu/CD@GE) (Fig. S4c), and mixed metal-modified CD@GE (Fig. S4d). The measurements were recorded using several scan rates, including 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 mV/s. The electrodes were subjected to measurements utilizing varying concentrations of Cu2+ ions (Figs. S5–S9). All electrodes exhibit identical characteristics when subjected to the same scan rate. The conductivity of CDs leads to an increase in the current of the GE electrode (Fig. S4). Similarly, the current of GE was enhanced by the interaction of Cu2+ ions with CDs (Fig. S4). The magnitude of the current response at a constant scan rate is contingent upon the concentration of Cu2+ ions (Figs. S5–S9). On the other hand, the presence of mixed metal ions has been seen to inhibit the flow of electric current in GE, resulting in a reduction in the current magnitude I (A/cm2, as depicted in Fig. S4).

The current response over the scan rate for all electrodes is quantitatively shown in Fig. 5 a. Both Cd2+ without and Cu2+ ions improve the current response of GE at all scan rates (Fig. 5a). On the other side, mixed metal ions at higher concentrations suppress the current values at all scan rates (Fig. 5a). While at lower concentrations of mixed metal ions, the current is higher than bare GE (Fig. 5a). This observation could be due to the interaction of the mixed metal ions with the electrolyte i.e., K3[Fe(CN)6]. These observations can be also supported by the area of the curves for electrodes at the same scan rate of 50 mV/s (Fig. 5b). Based on the current values of the electrodes, Cu2+/CDs@GE exhibits the highest response offering higher selectivity that supports the previous fluorescence measurements, vide supra (Fig. 5c).

Chronoamperometry (CA) measurements were conducted for all electrodes and the results are presented in Fig. 5d. They assessed the temporal evolution of responses for CD@GE, Cu/CDs@GE, and mixed metal/CDs@GE electrodes. Both electrodes, CD@GE and Cu/CDs@GE demonstrate similar current response values. On the other hand, the mixed metal/CDs@GE electrode (Fig. 5d) exhibits a decrease in the current response, which supports the findings obtained from the CV curves (Fig. 5a–c).

The LSV curves of the electrodes were documented in Fig. 6 a. Except for the combined metal/CDs@GE electrode, all other electrodes exhibit a comparable LSV curve (Fig. 6a). The formerly distinct peak of GE has become indiscernible, whereas a new peak at −0.25 V has been recorded. The alterations seen in the GE electrode's reaction can be attributed to the presence of metal ions, namely Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Na+. These metal ions interact with the electrolyte, leading to changes in the electrode's behavior.

EIS using Nyquist plots (Fig. 6b), as well as Bode plots (Fig. S10) were also recorded. The impedance of the GE electrode remains relatively similar when CDs and Cu@CDs are utilized (Fig. 6b). Similarly, the impedance value over frequency (Fig. 6b), and the phase over frequency (Fig. 6S10), also demonstrate little variation in the presence of CDs and Cu@CDs. In contrast, the impedance of bare GE exhibits an increase when exposed to mixed metal ions, (Fig. 6b). These observations explain the decrease in the current reported in other electrochemical measurements, such as CV, LSV, and CA tests.

The interaction of different Cu2+ ions concentrations with CDs deposited into the GE electrode was evaluated using electrochemical measurements of CV curves (Figs. S5–S9, and Fig. 6c), CA (Fig. 6d), LSV (Fig. 6e), and Nyquist plots (Fig. 6f). Before the complete saturation of CDs particles, a low concentration of Cu2+ ions causes a decrease of the CDs@GE current followed by an increase in the current at a higher concentration of Cu2+ ions. There is a significant change in the electrode response upon interaction with different Cu2+ ions.

There are several methods available for the identification and quantification of copper. Several commonly employed methodologies are frequently utilized in this context. Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) is a widely used analytical technique in scientific research that enables the determination of copper content in a given sample through the evaluation of its light absorption characteristics at a specific wavelength. The utilization of inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) is a method commonly applied in the determination of copper concentration in a given sample, with the measurement relying on the mass-to-charge ratio. The colorimetric test technique entails employing a chemical reaction to induce a chromatic alteration, which exhibits a linear relationship with the copper content in the provided sample. Biosensors are scientific devices that utilize biological molecules to detect the presence of copper. The choice of the detection method for copper will depend on the specific application. AAS or ICP-MS is a widely utilized analytical method for the identification and quantification of copper in water samples. Nevertheless, these methodologies are characterized by their high cost and advanced nature. On the contrary, fluorescence spectroscopy is characterized by its simplicity and cost-effectiveness.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were produced using a hydrothermal method using 3,4-aminobenzoic acid and hydrazine hydrate as precursors [69]. The CDs in question produce light at wavelengths of 490 nm (green) and 570 nm (yellow). The influence of medium pH significantly affects the relative intensity of the two emission bands, therefore establishing the materials as a dependable pH sensor within the range of 2.0–7.6. The ratiometric emission of CDs exhibits sensitivity towards trace quantities of Fe3+ ions, resulting in a reduction of emission intensity at 490 nm and an enhancement of emission intensity at 570 nm. The observed phenomenon involves a transition in color from a mild green hue to a vibrant yellow shade upon exposure to an ultraviolet (UV) lamp, which corresponds to an increase in the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The primary source of the ratiometric change in emission intensities is the complexation of CDs with Fe3+ ions and subsequent aggregation. Smartphones and solid-phase sensors are capable of detecting Fe3+ ions. CDs are utilized for the detection of intracellular pH and exogenous Fe3+ in MCF-7 breast cancer cells, exhibiting notable anticancer properties.

The production of N-CDs using 4-amino salicylic acid demonstrated the capability for selective detection and imaging of aluminum ions (Al3+) [70]. N-CDs was used for the analysis of Al3+ ions within live cells, while effectively mitigating the interference caused by autofluorescence. The achievement was facilitated by the exceptional selectivity and sensitivity exhibited by their detection technique in identifying Al3+ ions. The experimental procedure demonstrated a strong linear correlation throughout the concentration intervals of 10–200 μM and 225–400 μM. Additionally, the approach displayed a detection limit of 1.69 μM. Furthermore, a sensing strip has been fabricated by integrating N-CDs into a paper substrate to detect Al3+ ions. This approach presents a pragmatic and easily implementable technique for visually detecting the existence of Al3+ ions in a sample [70].

Cadmium sulfide (CdS) QDs into chitosan (CTS), denoted as CdS@CTS, was reported for heavy metal ions detection [60]. CdS@CTS QDs have the potential to be utilized for the visual identification of heavy metals, namely Cu2+ ions, because of their exceptional selectivity. It can offer a naked eye detection of Cu2+ ions [61]. The affinity is based on the cadmium substitution i.e. formation of Cd1-xCuxS and also interaction with CTS. The method can be used for qualitative analysis. CDs offer high computability compared to inorganic-based QDs such as CdS-modified chitosan (CdS@CTS) [60]. They are cadmium-free i.e. highly biocompatible.

This study has demonstrated the successful synthesis of N, S, and O-doped carbon dots (CDs) using a hydrothermal method utilizing l-cysteine as the precursor. The N, S, and O-doped CDs exhibit a blue fluorescence emission with a wavelength of 425 nm when excited with a wavelength of 350 nm. The CDs also demonstrate a discerning quenching effect specifically with Cu2+ ions when subjected to heavy metal ion interactions. This quenching effect is linear within the concentration range of 10–33.3 μM, with a minimal detectable concentration of 2 μM. The investigation of the fluorescence changes during interactions between N, S, O-doped CDs, and Cu2+ ions revealed that Cu2+ ions have a pronounced affinity for the outer surface of the CDs, resulting in the formation of selected aggregations. N, S, and O-doped CDs can also serve as probe for electrochemical measurements. The results of this study suggest that N, S, and O-doped CDs have the potential to be used as highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probes for the detection of heavy metal ions, particularly Cu2+ ions.

Faisal K. Algethami: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Hani Nasser Abdelhamid: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research & Innovation, Ministry of Education in Saudi Arabia for funding this research through the project number IFP-IMSIU-2023100. The authors also appreciate the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU) for supporting and supervising this project.
